title: Where the Jobs Are: The Disconnect in Entry-Level Roles
content:
Recent labor market data reveals a troubling disconnect between college majors and available entry-level job opportunities in the United States. Over the last two decades, college completions rose significantly, but the growth of suitable entry-level jobs failed to keep pace. The core issue is that what students are studying is completely out of sync with where the modern economy is creating jobs. In the majority of academic fields, the ratio of entry-level jobs per graduate has steadily declined. Experts warn that this marks the first time the traditional educational pathway to employment is genuinely broken.

The young are often the first to suffer when labor-saving technologies arrive or economic crises strike. The combination of high interest rates, changing tariffs, and the rapid rise of generative AI has created a stagnant hiring environment. This low-hire, low-fire dynamic has effectively halted the typical job churn that helps new graduates get a foot in the door. Since employers can easily find experienced workers to fill junior roles, many companies have paused their entry-level hiring. Consequently, new graduates face immense challenges trying to launch their professional careers.

Economists point out that the higher education system has been far too slow to course-correct in response to these trends. For years, universities failed to tailor their curriculums to the skills most demanded by employers. For example, while the health care sector has millions of job openings, the number of health-related college graduates has barely grown. Conversely, fields like computer and information sciences saw massive increases in graduates despite a collapse in entry-level openings. This massive misalignment leaves millions of students burdened with degrees that do not translate into immediate employment.
